does: This paper identifies and addresses instability in reward models used for RLHF by proposing an uncertainty-aware policy optimization algorithm. The authors empirically demonstrate inconsistencies across reward models trained on standard datasets and theoretically analyze how reward model fluctuations lead to policy overfitting and increased risk. They leverage concentration of measure bounds to develop a conservative algorithm that explicitly accounts for reward uncertainty during policy optimization, making learned policies more cautious toward uncertain rewards. The method is shown theoretically to reduce risk compared to standard RLHF approaches, with the key insight that policies overfitting to unreliable reward signals can fail catastrophically if those signals are incorrect.
connects: This work builds on the RLHF paradigm established by prior work on aligning language models with human preferences, extending recent findings that larger reward models and better data improve alignment. It responds to growing concerns about reward model reliability in alignment research, connecting to broader literature on uncertainty quantification in machine learning and safe reinforcement learning. The paper's use of concentration of measure for conservative policy optimization draws from robust optimization and distributionally robust RL, while the focus on overfitting in the alignment context extends classical statistical learning theory concerns to the high-stakes domain of LLM alignment where reward model errors are costly.
enables: This work opens pathways toward more reliable alignment procedures by establishing uncertainty quantification as a central component of RLHF. It enables development of adaptive algorithms that adjust exploration-exploitation tradeoffs based on reward confidence levels, and suggests future work on jointly improving reward model quality alongside policy learning under uncertainty. The framework could facilitate building alignment methods robust to reward model misspecification, supporting safer deployment of large language models. It also motivates investigation into how uncertainty estimates from reward models propagate through policy learning and how this framework scales to state-of-the-art language model sizes, potentially informing better practices for eliciting and aggregating human preferences.
